evidence_quotes.quote: The case centered on Epic's use of interface dark patterns that tricked players into unwanted purchases.

Blizzard's Diablo Immortal represents perhaps the most egregious example of predatory monetization in a major franchise, with estimates suggesting it could cost over $100,000 to fully upgrade a single character.

paragraphs.text: Epic Games' Fortnite faced unprecedented regulatory action from the FTC, resulting in a $520 million settlement - the largest administrative order in FTC history. The case centered on Epic's use of interface dark patterns that tricked players into unwanted purchases. (Quite a good Example if you ask me.) Also: Blizzard's Diablo Immortal represents perhaps the most egregious example of predatory monetization in a major franchise, with estimates suggesting it could cost over $100,000 to fully upgrade a single character. The game has been banned in several European countries due to its aggressive monetization PS: We need them to stop killing games. Back in my younger days we had games that had a developement cost of like 5-10 million like BF1942 or Battlefield 2 with 15 million in cost and had AI (Bots), Multiplayer and Singelplayer and are still playable to this Day and has an active Community even though bf2 was taken down officially its still active because it can run without verification of an online service like steam or Origin (EA) also the servers are hosted by just Hosting sevices or Users themselves. And now they come with: ah it costs too much Bullshit. in Short: (Battlefield 1942 and Battlefield 2 delivered robust value: Full offline features: Bots/AI, single-player modes, and LAN play. Dedicated servers: Anyone could host; when official support stopped, fans could still keep the game alive. No online verification DRM: The game itself ran independently of outside servers or services; you bought it, you owned it. Modding support: Communities created maps, mods, and whole new experiences, making games last for decades.) And what i hate: You buy a game, but it might die when the servers turn off or the company loses interest. "Many of the best, longest-lived gamesBF1942, BF2, Quake, StarCraft, Age of Empiressurvived because they were built to be owned and played by communities, not just rented from a company pushing microtransactions. If support costs too much, maybe the business model, not the game, is the problem."
